mappings.rationaleAI: The Library of Alexandria as the greatest ancient centre of learning, with its Mouseion research institution and text collection, addresses libraries (B.4) and academies of learning (C.2).
The Library of Ashurbanipal at Nineveh as the greatest collection of cuneiform texts addresses libraries (B.4) and archives (B.5) in the ancient world.
The invention and impact of the book from scrolls through codex to print addresses publishing history (C.3) and book publishing (C.3.a.i).
Caxton's introduction of the printing press to England and its impact on literacy and the book trade address publishing (C.3) and book publishing (C.3.a.i).
Pliny's Natural History as one of the first encyclopaedic works directly addresses encyclopaedias (B.2) as a form of knowledge storage and retrieval.
The origins of writing from cuneiform to alphabetic script enabled the storage and retrieval of information (B), foundational for all subsequent knowledge institutions.
Writing's role in disseminating scientific knowledge through printed texts and the Republic of Letters addresses publishing and the dissemination of knowledge (C.3).

The information technology revolution covers digital communication, the internet, and the transformation of media, connecting to databases (B.6), electronic publishing (C.3.b), and broadcasting (C.4).
Heritage culture covers the role of museums, historic preservation, and the politics of commemoration, addressing museums and galleries (A.1) and historic places and landmarks (A.3).

mappings.episodeTitle: The Royal Society and British Science: Episode 3
mappings.rationaleAI: The 19th-century proliferation of specialist scientific societies (Geological Society, Linnean Society, British Association) addresses academies of learning established for the advancement of knowledge (C.2).
Imperial science's use of botanical gardens (Kew) and natural history collections for colonial purposes addresses museums of natural history (A.1.b.ii) and botanical gardens (A.4.c).
